section.title: Hezekiah's Illness
section.sectionRationale: Hezekiah, told he will die, weeps and prays, and the LORD grants him fifteen more years along with a miraculous sign of the shadow moving backward. Yet his subsequent pride in showing Babylonian envoys all his treasures draws a sober prophecy from Isaiah that everything, and some of his own descendants, will one day be carried off to Babylon, a warning Hezekiah receives with troubling passivity.
section.thesis: The same king whose earnest prayer moved the LORD to extend his life and reverse a shadow on the sundial later shows a complacent pride that plants the seed of Babylon's coming exile, proving that answered prayer and continued faithfulness are not automatically the same thing.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Hezekiah's illness, prayer, and healing, verses 1 to 7; the sign of the shadow's reversal, verses 8 to 11; and the Babylonian envoys and Isaiah's sobering prophecy, verses 12 to 21.

verses.text: In those days Hezekiah became mortally ill. The prophet Isaiah son of Amoz came to him and said, "This is what the LORD says: 'Put your house in order, for you are about to die; you will not recover.'"
Then Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD, saying,
"Please, O LORD, remember how I have walked before You faithfully and with {{shalem|wholehearted}} devotion; I have done what is good in Your sight." And Hezekiah wept bitterly.
Before Isaiah had left the middle courtyard, the word of the LORD came to him, saying,

"Go back and tell Hezekiah the leader of My people that this is what the LORD, the God of your father David, says: 'I have heard your prayer; I have seen your {{dema|tears}}. I will surely heal you. On the third day from now you will go up to the house of the LORD.
I will add fifteen years to your life. And I will deliver you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria. I will defend this city for My sake and for the sake of My servant David.'"
Then Isaiah said, "Prepare a poultice of figs." So they brought it and applied it to the boil, and Hezekiah recovered.

units.label: The sign of the shadow's reversal
units.note: the shadow went back the ten steps

verses.text: Now Hezekiah had asked Isaiah, "What will be the {{oth|sign}} that the LORD will heal me and that I will go up to the house of the LORD on the third day?"
And Isaiah had replied, "This will be a sign to you from the LORD that He will do what He has promised: Would you like the shadow to go forward ten steps, or back ten steps?"
"It is easy for the shadow to lengthen ten steps," answered Hezekiah, "but not for it to go back ten steps."
So Isaiah the prophet called out to the LORD, and He brought the shadow back the ten steps it had descended on the stairway of Ahaz.

units.label: Babylonian envoys and Isaiah's warning
units.note: everything will be carried off to Babylon

verses.text: At that time Merodach-baladan son of Baladan king of Babylon sent letters and a gift to Hezekiah, for he had heard about Hezekiah's illness.

And Hezekiah received the envoys and showed them all that was in his treasure house, the silver, the gold, the spices, and the precious oil, as well as his armory, all that was found in his storehouses. There was nothing in his palace or in all his dominion that Hezekiah did not show them.
Then the prophet Isaiah went to King Hezekiah and asked, "Where did those men come from, and what did they say to you?" "They came from a distant land," Hezekiah replied, "from Babylon."
"What have they seen in your palace?" Isaiah asked. "They have seen everything in my palace," answered Hezekiah. "There is nothing among my treasures that I did not show them."
Then Isaiah said to Hezekiah, "Hear the word of the LORD:
The time will surely come when everything in your palace and all that your fathers have stored up until this day will be carried off to Babylon. Nothing will be left, says the LORD.
And some of your descendants, your own flesh and blood, will be taken away to be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon."
But Hezekiah said to Isaiah, "The word of the LORD that you have spoken is good." For he thought, "Will there not at least be {{shalom|peace}} and security in my lifetime?"

As for the rest of the acts of Hezekiah, along with all his might and how he constructed the pool and the tunnel to bring water into the city, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And Hezekiah rested with his fathers, and his son Manasseh reigned in his place.

shalem.gloss: Whole, complete, or wholehearted. Hezekiah's appeal that he has walked before the LORD with a shalem heart echoes the ideal repeatedly held up for Israel's kings, most notably contrasted with Solomon's divided heart in old age, 1 Kings 11:4, and forms the basis of his plea for mercy.

dema.gloss: Tears. The LORD's specific response, I have seen your dema, shows His tender attentiveness not merely to Hezekiah's words but to his visible grief, affirming that genuine, wept prayer moves God in a way that guarded or merely formal petition may not.

oth.gloss: Sign, a visible token confirming a divine promise. Hezekiah's request for an oth, and his specific choice of the harder sign, the shadow moving backward rather than forward, reflects a desire for unmistakable confirmation, distinguishing his request from unbelief and instead showing careful, engaged faith.

shalom.gloss: Peace or security. Hezekiah's private, self-focused thought after hearing Isaiah's warning of coming exile, will there not at least be peace and security in my lifetime, reveals a troubling complacency, content that judgment would be deferred rather than grieved that it was coming at all.

basic.context: Hezekiah, told plainly by Isaiah that his illness is fatal, turns to the wall and prays with tears, appealing to his wholehearted faithfulness before the LORD. Before Isaiah has even left the palace, the LORD answers, promising fifteen more years of life and deliverance from Assyria, confirmed by the sign of the sun's shadow miraculously moving backward. Yet later, when envoys from Babylon visit, Hezekiah proudly shows them everything in his treasuries and armory, prompting Isaiah to declare that everything Hezekiah possesses, and some of his own descendants, will eventually be carried off to Babylon. Strikingly, Hezekiah responds not with alarm but with quiet relief that this judgment will not fall in his own lifetime.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting a specific illness, healing, miraculous sign, and subsequent prophetic warning; it is not a general formula guaranteeing that earnest prayer always extends life, nor a specific ritual for treating illness. What endures is the pattern of God's genuine responsiveness to sincere, tearful prayer, and the sobering reminder that even a generally faithful believer can display troubling complacency about consequences that will fall on others after them.
basic.originalLanguages: Hezekiah appeals to his shalem, wholehearted, devotion, and the LORD explicitly responds not just to his words but to his dema, tears, showing genuine, wept prayer moves God. Hezekiah requests an oth, sign, choosing the harder and more unmistakable confirmation. Yet later, upon hearing of coming judgment, his private thought concerns only whether shalom, peace, would last through his own lifetime, revealing a complacency that stands in uncomfortable contrast to his earlier wholehearted devotion.
basic.typology: The miraculous reversal of the sun's shadow, defying the ordinary order of creation to confirm God's promise, anticipates the pattern of God's power overturning the expected natural or moral order entirely for the sake of His covenant purposes, ultimately fulfilled in the resurrection, which reverses death itself.
basic.christ: Hezekiah's earnest, tearful prayer for healing, answered before Isaiah had even left the courtyard, anticipates the pattern of Christ's own perfect prayers being always heard by the Father, John 11:41-42, while the added fifteen years granted for the sake of David's covenant anticipates how every gracious extension and deliverance in Scripture ultimately serves God's larger purpose of bringing forth the promised Son of David, Jesus Christ.
basic.god: God is tenderly attentive to sincere, tearful prayer, willing to extend life and even reverse the ordinary order of nature to confirm His promises to those who seek Him earnestly. Yet God also holds leaders responsible for pride and complacency, and Hezekiah's later self-satisfied response to Isaiah's warning of coming judgment on his descendants shows that even genuine past faithfulness does not exempt anyone from present accountability for new failures.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 38 records this same illness and healing in fuller poetic detail, including Hezekiah's own psalm of thanksgiving. 1 Kings 11:4 uses the same language of a wholehearted heart, there describing Solomon's tragic departure from it in old age, a contrast that frames Hezekiah's own claim to wholehearted devotion. 2 Kings 24 to 25 later narrates the exact Babylonian exile Isaiah warns of here, showing this brief episode planted seeds that would bear bitter fruit generations later.
basic.oneStory: Hezekiah's earnest, answered prayer for healing and the miraculous sign confirming it stand as a genuine testimony to God's attentive mercy, yet his later prideful display to Babylonian envoys, met with troubling complacency about the judgment it provokes, quietly plants the seed of the very exile that will one day engulf his own descendants, a sobering reminder that even the godliest life remains vulnerable to pride and that only Christ offers wholehearted faithfulness without any such lapse.

context.literary: The chapter's two halves deliberately contrast Hezekiah at his best, tearful, dependent prayer met with immediate mercy and a spectacular sign, with Hezekiah at his most troubling, prideful display met with a sobering prophecy he receives with a self-focused, complacent shrug.
context.historical: Merodach-baladan was a genuine historical Babylonian ruler who repeatedly rebelled against Assyrian control during this period, making his diplomatic outreach to Hezekiah, likely seeking an anti-Assyrian alliance, both historically plausible and politically significant. The stairway of Ahaz likely refers to a sundial-like structure using steps to mark the sun's shadow, a recognized ancient timekeeping device.
context.canonical: This chapter is closely paralleled in Isaiah 38 to 39, and Isaiah's prophecy of coming Babylonian exile here anticipates, with striking specificity nearly a century in advance, the very events narrated in 2 Kings 24 to 25, when Judah's treasures and even royal descendants are indeed carried off to Babylon.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show both the LORD's genuine responsiveness to Hezekiah's earnest, tearful prayer for healing, confirmed by a miraculous sign, and the troubling complacency Hezekiah later displayed toward the judgment his own pride provoked, planting the seed of Judah's eventual Babylonian exile.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical events, a particular illness, a specific requested sign, and a particular diplomatic visit; it does not prescribe that believers should request signs from God as standard practice, nor does it suggest that showing hospitality to foreign visitors is inherently wrong. What endures is the pattern of sincere prayer meeting God's mercy, and the warning against complacency regarding consequences that will affect others after us.

debates.question: Why does Hezekiah respond to Isaiah's warning of coming Babylonian exile with apparent relief rather than alarm or repentance, verse 19?

views.label: A genuinely troubling display of selfish complacency
views.case: Hezekiah's private thought, will there not at least be peace and security in my lifetime, reads as a self-centered relief that judgment is merely deferred rather than averted, showing a startling lack of concern for his own descendants' future suffering, a real moral failure the text seems to record without excusing.

views.label: An ancient Near Eastern expression of resigned acceptance of a sovereign, unappealable divine verdict
views.case: Some read Hezekiah's response as simply an honest, submissive acceptance that the LORD's judgment, once declared, was fixed and unappealable, similar to other biblical figures who receive hard prophetic words with resigned acknowledgment rather than false hope of reversal, without necessarily implying indifference to his descendants' fate.

debates.bearing: Whichever reading is preferred, the text's own bare, unembellished report of Hezekiah's private thought, offered without any explicit editorial praise, invites readers to weigh the tension between genuine submission to God's sovereign word and the danger of using that submission as cover for self-focused complacency.

study.originalLanguages: Hezekiah pleads on the basis of his shalem, wholehearted, devotion, and the LORD specifically notes He has seen his dema, tears, confirming that genuine, wept prayer moves God's heart. Hezekiah requests and receives an unmistakable oth, sign, choosing the more difficult confirmation of the shadow moving backward. Yet upon hearing Isaiah's warning of coming judgment, his private thought concerns only whether shalom, peace, would endure through his own lifetime, a troubling contrast to the wholehearted devotion he had claimed only verses earlier.

typology.type: The sun's shadow miraculously reversed to confirm God's promise
typology.fulfillment: God's power to overturn the expected natural order entirely for His covenant purposes, ultimately displayed in the resurrection's reversal of death itself, 1 Corinthians 15:54-57.

typology.type: A king's private complacency about judgment deferred to a later generation
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's call to genuine, others-focused repentance rather than mere personal relief at delayed consequences, 2 Corinthians 7:10.

study.christ: Hezekiah's earnest, tearful prayer, answered by the LORD before Isaiah had even left the courtyard, anticipates the pattern of Christ's own perfect prayers, always heard by the Father, John 11:41-42, and the miraculous sign of the reversed shadow, overturning the ordinary created order to confirm God's word, previews the far greater reversal accomplished in Christ's resurrection, which overturns death itself, 1 Corinthians 15:54-57. Yet where Hezekiah's later complacency about judgment falling on his descendants reveals the limits of even a generally faithful human king, Christ's perfect, wholehearted devotion to the Father never wavers, securing a salvation with no such troubling lapse for those who trust in Him.
study.god: God is tenderly attentive to sincere, tearful prayer, willing both to extend life and to reverse the ordinary order of creation itself to confirm His promises to those who earnestly seek Him. Yet God also holds even faithful leaders accountable for pride and complacency, and Hezekiah's troubling, self-focused response to news of coming judgment on his own descendants shows that past sincerity does not exempt anyone from present responsibility for new failures of the heart.

crossReferences.note: Records this same illness and healing with additional detail, including Hezekiah's own psalm of thanksgiving for his recovery.
Uses the same language of a wholehearted heart, there describing Solomon's tragic departure from it, framing Hezekiah's own claim to wholehearted devotion in this chapter.
Narrates the actual Babylonian exile Isaiah warns of here, showing this episode's prophecy fulfilled in devastating detail generations later.
Distinguishes godly sorrow that leads to repentance from mere relief that consequences are delayed, illuminating the tension in Hezekiah's own response.

study.oneStory: Hezekiah's earnest, tearful prayer for healing and the miraculous sign confirming God's promise stand as genuine testimony to the LORD's attentive mercy, yet his later prideful display before Babylonian envoys, met with troubling complacency rather than alarm at the judgment it provokes, quietly plants the very seed of the exile that will one day engulf his own descendants. This sobering juxtaposition reminds every reader that even a generally godly life remains vulnerable to pride, and that only Christ's perfectly wholehearted devotion to the Father secures a salvation with no such lapse.

quiz.q: What did Hezekiah do upon hearing Isaiah's initial declaration that he would die from his illness?

options.text: He turned his face to the wall, prayed, appealing to his wholehearted devotion before the LORD, and wept bitterly.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 record this exact, earnest, and tearful response.

options.text: He immediately accepted the news calmly with no emotional response.
options.note: The text explicitly describes intense weeping and earnest prayer, not calm acceptance.

options.text: He refused to believe Isaiah and dismissed the prophecy entirely.
options.note: Hezekiah took the prophecy seriously enough to pray urgently rather than dismissing it.

options.text: He immediately named his successor and made no appeal to God.
options.note: The text specifically describes prayer and weeping, not immediate succession planning.

quiz.q: What sign did Hezekiah request, and which option did he choose?

options.text: He was offered the choice between the shadow moving forward or backward ten steps, and he chose the more difficult sign, the shadow moving backward.
options.note: Verses 9 to 10 record this exact choice and Hezekiah's specific selection.

options.text: He requested no sign at all and simply trusted the promise.
options.note: The text explicitly records Hezekiah requesting a specific confirming sign in verse 8.

options.text: He asked for a sign involving rainfall rather than a shadow.
options.note: The specific sign concerned the sun's shadow on a stairway, not rainfall.

options.text: He chose the easier sign of the shadow moving forward.
options.note: The text explicitly states he chose the harder sign, the shadow moving backward.

quiz.q: What did Hezekiah do when envoys from Babylon visited him, and how did this provoke Isaiah's response?

options.text: He showed them everything in his treasure house and armory with nothing held back, prompting Isaiah to prophesy that it would all one day be carried off to Babylon.
options.note: Verses 13 to 17 record this exact display and Isaiah's resulting prophecy.

options.text: He refused to let the envoys see anything of value in his kingdom.
options.note: The text explicitly states he showed them everything, with nothing left unseen.

options.text: He sent the envoys away without receiving them at all.
options.note: The text explicitly states he received the envoys and gave them a full tour of his treasures.

options.text: Isaiah praised Hezekiah's hospitality without any warning.
options.note: Isaiah's response was a sober warning of coming judgment, not praise.

quiz.q: What specific judgment did Isaiah pronounce regarding Hezekiah's treasures and descendants?

options.text: Everything in the palace would eventually be carried off to Babylon, and some of Hezekiah's own descendants would be taken to serve as eunuchs in the Babylonian palace.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record this exact and specific prophecy.

options.text: Nothing would ever be lost from Hezekiah's treasury.
options.note: The prophecy explicitly states everything would eventually be carried away, the opposite of nothing being lost.

options.text: Only foreign nations, not Judah, would ultimately be affected.
options.note: The prophecy specifically concerns Judah's own treasures and Hezekiah's own descendants.

options.text: No specific prophecy was given regarding future consequences.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 give a very specific and detailed prophecy of future judgment.

quiz.q: How did Hezekiah respond to Isaiah's warning of coming Babylonian exile?

options.text: He accepted it as good, privately thinking there would at least be peace and security during his own lifetime.
options.note: Verse 19 records this exact, somewhat complacent response.

options.text: He immediately repented and pleaded for the judgment to be reversed.
options.note: The text records a notably passive acceptance rather than urgent pleading for reversal.

options.text: He became furious with Isaiah and had him imprisoned.
options.note: No such hostile reaction toward Isaiah is recorded; Hezekiah's response was verbal acceptance.

options.text: He denied that Isaiah had actually prophesied any such thing.
options.note: The text explicitly records his acknowledgment of the word as good, not a denial of it.

questions: Hezekiah's tearful, earnest prayer moved God to answer before the prophet had even left the room. What burden are you carrying that you have not yet brought to God with the same raw honesty and tears?

Hezekiah chose the harder sign, the shadow moving backward, rather than the easier one. Where might God be inviting you to trust Him for something more difficult to believe rather than settling for an easier confirmation?

Hezekiah's pride in showing off his treasures to impressive foreign visitors set in motion consequences he would not personally face. What is one area of pride in your own life that could have consequences for people who come after you?

Hezekiah's private relief that judgment would not fall in his own lifetime reveals a troubling complacency about the future of others. Where might you be tempted to feel relieved about a problem simply because it will not affect you directly?

This chapter shows the same man capable of both deeply earnest faith and troubling self-focus within a short span. How do you hold together the reality of your own genuine faith with honest awareness of your continuing capacity for pride or complacency?

translationNotes.note: The fifteen years added to Hezekiah's life are explicitly tied to the LORD's defense of Jerusalem for His own sake and for the sake of His covenant with David, linking this personal healing directly to the larger Davidic covenant promise.

The stairway of Ahaz likely functioned as a sundial-like structure, using the sun's shadow cast across its steps to mark the passage of time, an ancient timekeeping method whose miraculous reversal here defies the ordinary created order.

Merodach-baladan was a historically documented Babylonian ruler known for repeated rebellions against Assyrian dominance, making his diplomatic gift to Hezekiah plausibly connected to seeking an anti-Assyrian alliance.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 20:1-21. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
